concept.headline: The = operator is a match operator. It matches shapes and binds named variables. Destructure tuples, lists, and maps. _ ignores. ^ pins to the current value.
concept.explanation: In Elixir, = checks if the left side MATCHES the structure of the right side. If variables appear on the left, they are bound to the corresponding positions. {a, b} = {1, 2} binds a=1, b=2. [h | t] = [1,2,3] binds h=1, t=[2,3]. If the shape does not match, a MatchError is raised. _ (underscore) explicitly ignores a position. ^ (pin) prevents rebinding — ^x = 5 requires x's current value to be 5.
concept.syntax:
# Tuple destructure
{a, b} = {1, 2}   # a=1, b=2

# Tagged tuple
{:ok, val} = {:ok, 42}    # val=42

# List head/tail
[h | t] = [1, 2, 3]      # h=1, t=[2,3]

# Ignore with _
{_, b} = {1, 99}  # b=99, first ignored

# Pin operator
x = 5
^x = 5   # OK — current x is 5
^x = 6   # MatchError — 6 != 5

# Map pattern
%{name: n} = %{name: "Alice", age: 30}  # n='Alice'
keyPoints: = matches structure — raises MatchError if shapes differ
{:ok, val} = result — tests :ok AND binds val in one step
[h | t] = list — binds head to h, rest of list to t
^ pins to current value — match without rebinding

demo.intro: Watch = match and destructure tuples and lists, then see a MatchError when shapes differ.
demo.code:
{status, value} = {:ok, 42}
IO.puts(status)
IO.puts(value)

[first | rest] = [10, 20, 30]
IO.puts(first)
IO.inspect(rest)

{_, second} = {"ignore", "keep"}
IO.puts(second)

x = 7
^x = 7
IO.puts("pin matched")

explanation.what: {status, value} = {:ok, 42} — match a 2-tuple, bind status=:ok, value=42
explanation.why: Left side is a tuple pattern with two variable positions
explanation.intent: Show tuple destructuring — binding multiple variables in one match
explanation.misconception: This is NOT Python tuple unpacking even though the syntax looks similar. It is pattern MATCHING — if the right side were {:ok, 42, 'extra'} it would fail with MatchError because the shapes differ (2-tuple vs 3-tuple).

explanation.what: IO.puts(status) → ok (the atom :ok printed without colon)
explanation.why: status was bound to :ok by the pattern match
explanation.misconception: status is an atom :ok. IO.puts prints it as 'ok' without the colon.

explanation.why: value was bound to 42 by the pattern match
explanation.misconception: The {:ok, val} pattern is SO common in Elixir that it is the standard way to check for success. File.read('path') returns {:ok, contents} on success or {:error, reason} on failure.

explanation.why: [h | t] pattern splits a list into its first element (h) and remaining elements (t)
explanation.intent: Show | (cons) pattern for list head/tail splitting
explanation.misconception: rest is a LIST [20, 30] — not the single element 20. The | splits ONE element from the front; everything else is the tail list. [first | rest] on a single-element list [10] gives first=10, rest=[].

explanation.why: first was bound to the head element 10
explanation.misconception: The head is always ONE element — not a list. first=10 is an integer, not [10].

explanation.why: rest was bound to the tail [20, 30]
explanation.intent: Confirm tail binding is a list
explanation.misconception: IO.inspect is used here to show the list representation. IO.puts on a list of integers would fail — IO.inspect handles any data type.

explanation.why: _ matches anything but does NOT bind the value
explanation.intent: Show _ as an explicit 'I don't care' marker
explanation.misconception: Using _ (single underscore) is a SIGNAL to other programmers that you intentionally do not need this value. Using a real variable name (like x) but never using it is less clear.
You can use multiple _ in one pattern: {_, _, third} = {1, 2, 3} binds only third. You cannot use _ as a variable — IO.puts(_) would be an error.

explanation.what: x = 7 — binding x for the pin demonstration
explanation.why: x needs a current value before pinning
explanation.misconception: After x = 7, x is bound to 7. The next line ^x = 7 will TEST whether 7 == 7 — not rebind x.

explanation.what: ^x = 7 — pin operator: use x's CURRENT value (7) in the match. 7 == 7 → success.
explanation.why: ^ prevents rebinding — it forces the match to test against the current binding
explanation.change: x is NOT rebound. Match succeeds.
explanation.misconception: Without ^, x = 7 would REBIND x (even if x was already 7 — it would create a new binding). With ^x = 7, Elixir uses x's current value (7) as a literal to match against. ^x = 6 would raise MatchError because 6 != 7.

explanation.why: ^x = 7 matched because x's current value is 7
explanation.misconception: The pin operator is commonly used in case clauses and function heads when you need to match against a runtime variable value, not just a literal.

prediction.question:
What is the output?

[a, b | _] = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
IO.puts(a)
IO.puts(b)
prediction.explanation: [a, b | _] matches: a=1, b=2, rest=[3,4,5] (discarded by _). Output: 1 then 2.

explanation.afterDemo: Pattern matching is the single most important concept in Elixir. It appears everywhere: = operator, case clauses, function heads, with expressions, receive blocks. Mastering pattern matching means being able to write concise, declarative code that simultaneously validates structure and extracts data. {:ok, val} = result is more expressive than if result.status == :ok; val = result.value — it tests and extracts in one step.
keyTakeaways: = matches shapes. Variables bind. Fixed values (atoms, literals) must match exactly.
[h | t] splits a list into head and tail. _ explicitly ignores a position.
^var pins to the current binding — tests without rebinding.

practice.prompt: Pattern match {:error, code, msg} = {:error, 404, "not found"}. Print code and msg on separate lines.
practice.hint: 3-element tuple — :error must match exactly, code and msg are bound.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! code=404, msg='not found' extracted from the tuple.

summary.title: Pattern Matching — Complete
takeaways: = matches structure. Mismatch → MatchError. Variables bind to positions.
[h | t] — head/tail destructure. _ ignores. ^var pins to current value.
{:ok, val} — idiomatic result destructure. Tests shape AND extracts data simultaneously.
